title: Historian

infobox.value: is a scholar who studies and writes about [the past](/wiki/History "History") and is regarded as an authority on it.[[1]](#cite_note-wordnetprinceton-1) Historians are concerned with the continuous, methodical narrative and research of past events as relating to the human race; as well as the study of all history in time. Some historians are recognized by publications or training and experience.[[2]](#cite_note-HermanAM-2) "Historian" became a professional occupation in the late nineteenth century as research universities were emerging in Germany. The professionalization process spread to Japan, the Netherlands, Italy, Spain, Russia, Scandinavia, Greece, Romania, and Latin America, with each adapting the German model with local modifications.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Scholar who deals with the exploration and presentation of history

For other uses, see [Historian (disambiguation)](/wiki/Historian_(disambiguation) "Historian (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Herodotos_Met_91.8.jpg/250px-Herodotos_Met_91.8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Herodotos_Met_91.8.jpg)

[Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") (c. 484 – c. 425 BC) was a Greek historian who lived in the fifth century BC and one of the earliest historians whose work survives.

A **historian** is a scholar who studies and writes about [the past](/wiki/History "History") and is regarded as an authority on it.[[1]](#cite_note-wordnetprinceton-1) Historians are concerned with the continuous, methodical narrative and research of past events as relating to the human race; as well as the study of all history in time. Some historians are recognized by publications or training and experience.[[2]](#cite_note-HermanAM-2) "Historian" became a professional occupation in the late nineteenth century as research universities were emerging in Germany. The professionalization process spread to Japan, the Netherlands, Italy, Spain, Russia, Scandinavia, Greece, Romania, and Latin America, with each adapting the German model with local modifications.

## Objectivity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Objectivity")]

### Among historians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Among historians")]

#### Ancient historians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancient historians")]

In the 19th century, scholars used to study ancient Greek and Roman historians to see how generally reliable they were. In recent decades, however, scholars have focused more on the constructions, genres, and meanings that ancient historians sought to convey to their audiences.[[3]](#cite_note-Marincola-3) History is always written with contemporary concerns and ancient historians wrote their histories in response to the needs of their times.[[3]](#cite_note-Marincola-3) Out of thousands of Greek and Roman historians, only the tiniest fraction's works survive and it is out of this small pool that ancient historians and ancient historiography are analyzed today.[[3]](#cite_note-Marincola-3) Modern historians of the ancient world have to deal with diverse types of evidence, which are debated more today than in the 19th century due to innovations in the field.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Ancient historians were very different from modern historians in terms of goals, documentation, sources, and methods.[[5]](#cite_note-Nicolai-5) For instance, chronological systems were not widely used, their sources were often absorbed (traceability of such sources usually disappeared), and the goal of an ancient work was often to create political or military paradigms. It was only after the emergence of Christianity that philosophies of history grew in prominence due to the destiny of man from the Christian account.[[5]](#cite_note-Nicolai-5) Epics such as [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer")'s works were used by historians and considered history even by [Thucydides](/wiki/Thucydides "Thucydides").[[5]](#cite_note-Nicolai-5) Ancient orators like Cicero stated that their historiographical narrative was divided into 3 divisions: history, argument, and fable.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

#### Modern historians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern historians")]

In the 19th-century historical studies became professionalized at universities and research centers along with a belief that history was a type of science.[[7]](#cite_note-iggers-7) However, in the 20th century historians incorporated social science dimensions like politics, economy, and culture in their historiography, including postmodernism.[[7]](#cite_note-iggers-7) Since the 1980s there has been a special interest in the memories and commemoration of past events.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

History by its nature is prone to continuous debate, and historians tend to be divided.[[9]](#cite_note-9) There is no past that is commonly agreed upon, since there are competing histories (e.g., of elites, non-elites, men, women, races, etc.).[[10]](#cite_note-10) It is widely accepted that "strict objectivity is epistemologically unattainable for historians".[[11]](#cite_note-11) Historians rarely articulate their conception of objectivity or discuss it in detail.[[12]](#cite_note-12) And like in other professions, historians rarely analyze themselves or their activity.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In practice, "specific canons of historical proof are neither widely observed nor generally agreed upon" among professional historians.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Though objectivity is often seen as the goal of those who work on history, in practice there is no convergence on anything in particular.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Historical scholarship is never value free since historian's writings are impacted by the frameworks of their times.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Some scholars of history have observed that there are no particular standards for historical fields such as religion, art, science, democracy, and social justice as these are by their nature 'essentially contested' fields, such that they require diverse tools particular to each field beforehand in order to interpret topics from those fields.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

There are three commonly held reasons why avoiding bias is not seen as possible in historical practice: a historian's interest inevitably influences their judgement (what information to use and omit, how to present the information, etc.); the sources used by historians for their history all have bias, and historians are products of their culture, concepts, and beliefs.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Racial and cultural biases can play major roles in national histories, which often ignore or downplay the roles on other groups.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Gender biases as well.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Moral or worldview evaluations by historians are also seen partly inevitable, causing complications for historians and their historical writings.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) One way to deal with this is for historians to state their biases explicitly for their readers.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In the modern era, newspapers (which have a bias of their own) impacts historical accounts made by historians.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Wikipedia also contributes to difficulties for historians.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Legal cases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Legal cases")]

During the *[Irving v Penguin Books and Lipstadt](/wiki/Irving_v_Penguin_Books_Ltd "Irving v Penguin Books Ltd")* trial, the [High Court of Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice") relied on Richard Evan's witness report which mentioned "objective historian" in the same vein as the [reasonable person](/wiki/Reasonable_person "Reasonable person"), and reminiscent of the standard traditionally used in English law of "[the man on the Clapham omnibus](/wiki/The_man_on_the_Clapham_omnibus "The man on the Clapham omnibus")".[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider20011531-27) This was necessary so that there would be a legal benchmark to compare and contrast the scholarship of an objective historian against the illegitimate methods employed by [David Irving](/wiki/David_Irving "David Irving"), as before the *Irving v Penguin Books and Lipstadt* trial, there was no legal precedent for what constituted an objective historian.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider20011531-27)

[Justice Gray](/wiki/Charles_Gray_(English_judge) "Charles Gray (English judge)") leant heavily on the research of one of the expert witnesses, [Richard J. Evans](/wiki/Richard_J._Evans "Richard J. Evans"), who compared [illegitimate distortion of the historical record](/wiki/Historical_revisionism_(negationism)#Techniques "Historical revisionism (negationism)") practiced by [Holocaust deniers](/wiki/Holocaust_denier "Holocaust denier") with established historical methodologies.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider20011534-28)

By summarizing Gray's judgment, in an article published in the *[Yale Law Journal](/wiki/Yale_Law_Journal "Yale Law Journal")*, Wendie E. Schneider distils these seven points for what he meant by an objective historian:[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider20011534,_1535-29)

> 1. The historian must treat sources with appropriate reservations;
> 2. The historian must not dismiss counter-evidence without scholarly consideration;
> 3. The historian must be even-handed in treatment of evidence and eschew "cherry-picking";
> 4. The historian must clearly indicate any speculation;
> 5. The historian must not mistranslate documents or mislead by omitting parts of documents;
> 6. The historian must weigh the authenticity of all accounts, not merely those that contradict their favored view; and
> 7. The historian must take the motives of historical actors into consideration.

Schneider uses the concept of the "objective historian" to suggest that this could be an aid in assessing what makes a historian suitable as expert witnesses under the [Daubert standard](/wiki/Daubert_standard "Daubert standard") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). Schneider proposed this, because, in her opinion, Irving could not have passed the standard Daubert tests unless a court was given "a great deal of assistance from historians".[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider20011534,_1538-30)

Schneider proposes that by testing a historian against the criteria of the "objective historian" then, even if a historian holds specific political views (and she gives an example of a well-qualified historian's testimony that was disregarded by a United States court because he was a member of a feminist group), providing the historian uses the "objective historian" standards, they are a "conscientious historian". It was Irving's failure as an "objective historian" not his right-wing views that caused him to lose his libel case, as a "conscientious historian" would not have "deliberately misrepresented and manipulated historical evidence" to support his political views.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider200115333,_1539-31)

## History analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History analysis")]

Main article: [Historical method](/wiki/Historical_method "Historical method")

The process of historical analysis involves investigation and analysis of competing ideas, facts, and purported facts to create coherent [narratives](/wiki/Narrative "Narrative") that explain "what happened" and "why or how it happened". Modern historical analysis usually draws upon other social sciences, including [economics](/wiki/Economics "Economics"), [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology"), [politics](/wiki/Politics "Politics"), [psychology](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology"), [anthropology](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology"), [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy"), and [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"). While ancient writers do not normally share modern historical practices, their work remains valuable for its insights within the cultural context of the times. An important part of the contribution of many modern historians is the verification or dismissal of earlier historical accounts through reviewing newly discovered sources and recent scholarship or through parallel disciplines like [archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology").

## Historiography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Historiography")]

Main article: [Historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography")

### Ancient

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ancient")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Thucydides_Manuscript.jpg/250px-Thucydides_Manuscript.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thucydides_Manuscript.jpg)

Reproduction of part of a tenth-century copy of [Thucydides](/wiki/Thucydides "Thucydides")'s *[History of the Peloponnesian War](/wiki/History_of_the_Peloponnesian_War "History of the Peloponnesian War")*.

Understanding the past appears to be a universal human need, and the telling of history has emerged independently in civilizations around the world. What constitutes history is a philosophical question (see [philosophy of history](/wiki/Philosophy_of_history "Philosophy of history")). The earliest [chronologies](/wiki/Chronology "Chronology") date back to [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") and [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), though no historical writers in these early civilizations were known by name.

[Systematic historical thought](/wiki/Greek_historiography "Greek historiography") emerged in [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), a development that became an important influence on the writing of history elsewhere around [the Mediterranean](/wiki/History_of_the_Mediterranean_region "History of the Mediterranean region") region. The earliest known critical historical works were *[The Histories](/wiki/Histories_(Herodotus) "Histories (Herodotus)")*, composed by [Herodotus of Halicarnassus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") (484 – c. 425 [BCE](/wiki/Before_Common_Era "Before Common Era")) who later became known as the "father of history" ([Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero")). Herodotus attempted to distinguish between more and less reliable accounts and personally conducted research by travelling extensively, giving written accounts of various [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Basin "Mediterranean Basin") cultures. Although Herodotus' overall emphasis lay on the actions and characters of men, he also attributed an important role to divinity in the determination of historical events. [Thucydides](/wiki/Thucydides "Thucydides") largely eliminated divine causality in his account of the war between Athens and Sparta, establishing a rationalistic element that set a precedent for subsequent Western historical writings. He was also the first to distinguish between cause and immediate origins of an event, while his successor [Xenophon](/wiki/Xenophon "Xenophon") (c. 431 – 355 BCE) introduced autobiographical elements and character studies in his [*Anabasis*](/wiki/Anabasis_(Xenophon) "Anabasis (Xenophon)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Leonardo_Bruni_-_Imagines_philologorum.jpg/250px-Leonardo_Bruni_-_Imagines_philologorum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_Bruni_-_Imagines_philologorum.jpg)

[Leonardo Bruni](/wiki/Leonardo_Bruni "Leonardo Bruni") (c. 1370–1444), the historian who first divided history into the three eras of [Antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history"), the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), and [Modern times](/wiki/Timelines_of_modern_history "Timelines of modern history").

The [Romans adopted the Greek tradition](/wiki/Roman_historiography "Roman historiography"). While early Roman works were still written in Greek, the *Origines*, composed by the Roman statesman [Cato the Elder](/wiki/Cato_the_Elder "Cato the Elder") (234–149 BCE), was written in Latin, in a conscious effort to counteract Greek cultural influence. [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") (63 BCE – c. 24 [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era")) was an important exponent of the Greco-Roman tradition of combining geography with history, presenting a descriptive history of peoples and places known to his era. [Livy](/wiki/Livy "Livy") (59 BCE – 17 CE) records the rise of [Rome](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") from [city-state](/wiki/City-state "City-state") to [empire](/wiki/Empire "Empire"). His speculation about what would have happened if [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") had marched against Rome represents the first known instance of [alternate history](/wiki/Alternate_history "Alternate history").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In [Chinese historiography](/wiki/Chinese_historiography "Chinese historiography"), the *[Classic of History](/wiki/Classic_of_History "Classic of History")* is one of the [Five Classics](/wiki/Five_Classics "Five Classics") of [Chinese classic texts](/wiki/Chinese_classic_texts "Chinese classic texts") and one of the earliest narratives of China. The *[Spring and Autumn Annals](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_Annals "Spring and Autumn Annals")*, the official chronicle of the State of Lu covering the period from 722 to 481 BCE, is among the earliest surviving Chinese historical texts arranged on [annalistic](/wiki/Annals "Annals") principles. [Sima Qian](/wiki/Sima_Qian "Sima Qian") (around 100 BCE) was the first in China to lay the groundwork for professional historical writing. His written work was the *[Shiji](/wiki/Shiji "Shiji")* (*[Records of the Grand Historian](/wiki/Records_of_the_Grand_Historian "Records of the Grand Historian")*), a monumental lifelong achievement in literature. Its scope extends as far back as the 16th century BCE, and it includes many treatises on specific subjects and individual biographies of prominent people and also explores the lives and deeds of commoners, both contemporary and those of previous eras.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Beda_Petersburgiensis_f3v.jpg/250px-Beda_Petersburgiensis_f3v.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beda_Petersburgiensis_f3v.jpg)

A page of [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede")'s *Ecclesiastical History of the English People*

Christian historiography began early, perhaps as early as [Luke-Acts](/wiki/Luke-Acts "Luke-Acts"), which is the [primary source](/wiki/Primary_source "Primary source") for the [Apostolic Age](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age"). Writing history was popular among Christian monks and clergy in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). They wrote about the history of Jesus Christ, that of the Church and that of their patrons, the dynastic history of the local rulers. In the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_medieval "Early medieval") historical writing often took the form of [annals](/wiki/Annals "Annals") or [chronicles](/wiki/Chronicle "Chronicle") recording events year by year, but this style tended to hamper the analysis of events and causes.[[34]](#cite_note-34) An example of this type of writing is the [Anglo-Saxon Chronicles](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle"), which were the work of several different writers: it was started during the reign of [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") in the late ninth century, but one copy was still being updated in 1154.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Muslim historical writings](/wiki/Historiography_of_early_Islam "Historiography of early Islam") first began to develop in the seventh century, with the reconstruction of the Prophet [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad")'s life in the centuries following his death. With numerous conflicting narratives regarding Muhammad and his [companions](/wiki/Sahaba "Sahaba") from various sources, scholars had to verify which sources were more reliable. To evaluate these sources, they developed various methodologies, such as the *[science of biography](/wiki/Ilm_ar-Rijal "Ilm ar-Rijal")*, *[science of hadith](/wiki/Science_of_hadith "Science of hadith")* and *[Isnad](/wiki/Isnad "Isnad")* (chain of transmission). They later applied these methodologies to other historical figures in the [Islamic civilization](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age"). Famous historians in this tradition include [Urwah](/wiki/Urwah_ibn_Zubayr "Urwah ibn Zubayr") (d. 712), [Wahb ibn Munabbih](/wiki/Wahb_ibn_Munabbih "Wahb ibn Munabbih") (d. 728), [Ibn Ishaq](/wiki/Ibn_Ishaq "Ibn Ishaq") (d. 761), [al-Waqidi](/wiki/Al-Waqidi "Al-Waqidi") (745–822), [Ibn Hisham](/wiki/Ibn_Hisham "Ibn Hisham") (d. 834), [Muhammad al-Bukhari](/wiki/Muhammad_al-Bukhari "Muhammad al-Bukhari") (810–870) and [Ibn Hajar](/wiki/Ibn_Hajar_Asqalani "Ibn Hajar Asqalani") (1372–1449).

### Enlightenment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Enlightenment")]

During the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), the modern development of historiography through the application of scrupulous methods began.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Voltaire-Baquoy.gif/250px-Voltaire-Baquoy.gif)](/wiki/File:Voltaire-Baquoy.gif)

[Voltaire](/wiki/Voltaire "Voltaire")'s works of history are an excellent example of [Enlightenment era](/wiki/Enlightenment_era "Enlightenment era") history writing. Painting by [Pierre Charles Baquoy](/wiki/Pierre_Charles_Baquoy "Pierre Charles Baquoy").

French *[philosophe](/wiki/Philosophes "Philosophes")* [Voltaire](/wiki/Voltaire "Voltaire") (1694–1778) had an enormous influence on the art of history writing. His best-known histories are *The Age of Louis XIV* (1751), and *Essay on the Customs and the Spirit of the Nations* (1756). "My chief object," he wrote in 1739, "is not political or military history, it is the history of the arts, of commerce, of civilization – in a word, – of the human mind."[[35]](#cite_note-35) He broke from the tradition of narrating diplomatic and military events, and emphasized customs, social history, and achievements in the arts and sciences. He was the first scholar to make a serious attempt to write the history of the world, eliminating theological frameworks, and emphasizing economics, culture, and political history.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Edward_Gibbon_by_Henry_Walton_cleaned.jpg/250px-Edward_Gibbon_by_Henry_Walton_cleaned.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_Gibbon_by_Henry_Walton_cleaned.jpg)

[Edward Gibbon](/wiki/Edward_Gibbon "Edward Gibbon")'s *[Decline of the Roman Empire](/wiki/The_History_of_the_Decline_and_Fall_of_the_Roman_Empire "The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire")* (1776) was a masterpiece of late 18th-century history writing.

At the same time, philosopher [David Hume](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume") was having a similar impact on history in [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"). In 1754, he published the *[History of England](/wiki/The_History_of_England_(David_Hume) "The History of England (David Hume)")*, a six-volume work that extended from the Invasion of Julius Caesar to the Revolution in 1688. Hume adopted a similar scope to Voltaire in his history; as well as the history of Kings, Parliaments, and armies, he examined the history of culture, including literature and science, as well.[[36]](#cite_note-36) [William Robertson](/wiki/William_Robertson_(historian) "William Robertson (historian)"), a Scottish historian, and the [Historiographer Royal](/wiki/Historiographer_Royal_(Scotland) "Historiographer Royal (Scotland)")[[37]](#cite_note-37) published the *History of Scotland 1542 – 1603*, in 1759 and his most famous work, *The history of the reign of [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor")* in 1769.[[38]](#cite_note-38) His scholarship was painstaking for the time and he was able to access a large number of documentary sources that had previously been unstudied. He was also one of the first historians who understood the importance of general and universally applicable ideas in the shaping of historical events.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The apex of Enlightenment history was reached with [Edward Gibbon](/wiki/Edward_Gibbon "Edward Gibbon")'s, monumental six-volume work, *[The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire](/wiki/The_History_of_the_Decline_and_Fall_of_the_Roman_Empire "The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire")*, published on 17 February 1776. Because of its relative objectivity and heavy use of [primary sources](/wiki/Primary_source "Primary source"), at the time its methodology became a model for later historians. This has led to Gibbon being called the first "modern historian".[[40]](#cite_note-40) The book sold impressively, earning its author a total of about £9000. Biographer [Leslie Stephen](/wiki/Sir_Leslie_Stephen "Sir Leslie Stephen") wrote that thereafter, "His fame was as rapid as it has been lasting."

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 19th century")]

The tumultuous events surrounding the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") inspired much of the historiography and analysis of the early 19th century. Interest in the 1688 [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") was also rekindled by the [Great Reform Act](/wiki/Great_Reform_Act "Great Reform Act") of 1832 in England.

[Thomas Carlyle](/wiki/Thomas_Carlyle "Thomas Carlyle") published his magnum opus, the three-volume *[The French Revolution: A History](/wiki/The_French_Revolution:_A_History "The French Revolution: A History")* in 1837.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-LL-42) The resulting work had a passion new to historical writing. [Thomas Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Macaulay "Thomas Macaulay") produced his most famous work of history, *[The History of England from the Accession of James the Second](/wiki/The_History_of_England_from_the_Accession_of_James_the_Second "The History of England from the Accession of James the Second")*, in 1848.[[43]](#cite_note-43) His writings are famous for their ringing prose and for their confident, sometimes dogmatic, emphasis on a progressive model of British history, according to which the country threw off superstition, autocracy and confusion to create a balanced constitution and a forward-looking culture combined with the freedom of belief and expression. This model of human progress has been called the [Whig interpretation of history](/wiki/Whig_history "Whig history").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Jules_Michelet_portrait_older.jpg/250px-Jules_Michelet_portrait_older.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jules_Michelet_portrait_older.jpg)

Jules Michelet, later in his career.

In his main work *Histoire de France*, French historian [Jules Michelet](/wiki/Jules_Michelet "Jules Michelet") coined the term [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") (meaning "Re-birth" in [French language](/wiki/French_language "French language")), as a period in Europe's cultural history that represented a break from the Middle Ages, creating a modern understanding of humanity and its place in the world.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
The nineteen-volume work covered French history from [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") to the outbreak of the [Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). Michelet was one of the first historians to shift the emphasis of history to the common people, rather than the leaders and institutions of the country. Another important French historian of the period was [Hippolyte Taine](/wiki/Hippolyte_Taine "Hippolyte Taine"). He was the chief theoretical influence of French [naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(literature) "Naturalism (literature)"), a major proponent of [sociological positivism](/wiki/Sociological_positivism "Sociological positivism") and one of the first practitioners of [historicist](/wiki/Historicism "Historicism") criticism. Literary historicism as a critical movement has been said to originate with him.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

One of the major progenitors of the history of [culture](/wiki/Cultural_history "Cultural history") and [art](/wiki/Art_history "Art history"), was the Swiss historian [Jacob Burckhardt](/wiki/Jacob_Burckhardt "Jacob Burckhardt")[[47]](#cite_note-47) Burckhardt's best-known work is *[The Civilization of the Renaissance in Italy](/wiki/The_Civilization_of_the_Renaissance_in_Italy "The Civilization of the Renaissance in Italy")* (1860). According to [John Lukacs](/wiki/John_Lukacs "John Lukacs"), he was the first master of cultural history, which seeks to describe the spirit and the forms of expression of a particular age, a particular people, or a particular place.[[48]](#cite_note-48) By the mid-19th century, scholars were beginning to analyse the history of institutional change, particularly the development of constitutional government. [William Stubbs](/wiki/William_Stubbs "William Stubbs")'s *Constitutional History of England* (3 vols., 1874–78) was an important influence on this developing field. The work traced the development of the English constitution from the Teutonic invasions of Britain until 1485, and marked a distinct step in the advance of English historical learning.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") introduced the concept of [historical materialism](/wiki/Historical_materialism "Historical materialism") into the study of world-historical development. In his conception, the economic conditions and dominant modes of production determined the structure of society at that point. Previous historians had focused on the cyclical events of the rise and decline of rulers and nations. Process of [nationalization of history](/wiki/Nationalization_of_history "Nationalization of history"), as part of [national revivals](/wiki/Romantic_nationalism "Romantic nationalism") in the 19th century, resulted with separation of "one's own" history from common [universal history](/wiki/Universal_history_(genre) "Universal history (genre)") by such way of perceiving, understanding and treating the past that constructed history as history of a nation.[[50]](#cite_note-laboratory-50) A new discipline, [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology"), emerged in the late 19th century and analyzed and compared these perspectives on a larger scale.

### Professionalization in Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Professionalization in Germany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Leopold_Von_Ranke_1877.jpg/250px-Leopold_Von_Ranke_1877.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leopold_Von_Ranke_1877.jpg)

[Ranke](/wiki/Leopold_von_Ranke "Leopold von Ranke") established history as a professional academic discipline in Germany.

The modern academic study of history and methods of historiography were pioneered in 19th-century German universities. [Leopold von Ranke](/wiki/Leopold_von_Ranke "Leopold von Ranke") was a pivotal influence in this regard, and is considered as the founder of modern source-based [history](/wiki/History "History").[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Specifically, he implemented the seminar teaching method in his classroom and focused on archival research and analysis of historical documents. Beginning with his first book in 1824, the *History of the Latin and Teutonic Peoples from 1494 to 1514*, Ranke used an unusually wide variety of sources for a historian of the age, including "memoirs, diaries, personal and formal missives, government documents, diplomatic dispatches and first-hand accounts of eye-witnesses". Over a career that spanned much of the century, Ranke set the standards for much of later historical writing, introducing such ideas as reliance on [primary sources](/wiki/Primary_source "Primary source") ([empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism")), an emphasis on [narrative history](/wiki/Narrative_history "Narrative history") and especially international politics (*[aussenpolitik](/wiki/Political_history "Political history")*).[[55]](#cite_note-55) Sources had to be hard, not speculations and rationalizations. His credo was to write history the way it was. He insisted on primary sources with proven authenticity.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 20th century")]

The term [Whig history](/wiki/Whig_history "Whig history") was coined by [Herbert Butterfield](/wiki/Herbert_Butterfield "Herbert Butterfield") in his short book *The Whig Interpretation of History* in 1931, (a reference to the British [Whigs](/wiki/Whig_(British_political_party) "Whig (British political party)"), advocates of the power of [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom")) to refer to the approach to historiography that presents the past as an inevitable progression towards ever greater [liberty](/wiki/Liberty "Liberty") and [enlightenment](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/enlightenment "wikt:enlightenment"), culminating in modern forms of [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") and [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"). In general, Whig historians emphasized the rise of [constitutional government](/wiki/Constitutional_government "Constitutional government"), [personal freedoms](/wiki/Personal_freedom "Personal freedom"), and [scientific progress](/wiki/Scientific_progress "Scientific progress"). The term has been also applied widely in historical disciplines outside of [British history](/wiki/British_history "British history") (the [history of science](/wiki/History_of_science "History of science"), for example) to criticize any [teleological](/wiki/Teleology "Teleology") (or goal-directed), hero-based, and [transhistorical](/wiki/Transhistoricity "Transhistoricity") narrative.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Butterfield's antidote to Whig history was "...to evoke a certain sensibility towards the past, the sensibility which studies the past 'for the sake of the past', which delights in the concrete and the complex, which 'goes out to meet the past', which searches for 'unlikenesses between past and present'."[[58]](#cite_note-58) Butterfield's formulation received much attention, and the kind of historical writing he argued against in generalised terms is no longer academically respectable.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Marc_Bloch.jpg/250px-Marc_Bloch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marc_Bloch.jpg)

The 20th century saw the creation of a huge variety of historiographical approaches. [Marc Bloch](/wiki/Marc_Bloch "Marc Bloch")'s focus on social history rather than traditional political history was of tremendous influence.

The French [Annales School](/wiki/Annales_School "Annales School") radically changed the focus of historical research in France during the 20th century by stressing long-term social history, rather than political or diplomatic themes. The school emphasized the use of quantification and the paying of special attention to geography.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) An eminent member of this school, [Georges Duby](/wiki/Georges_Duby "Georges Duby"), described his approach to history as one that

> relegated the sensational to the sidelines and was reluctant to give a simple accounting of events, but strived on the contrary to pose and solve problems and, neglecting surface disturbances, to observe the long and medium-term evolution of economy, society, and civilisation.

[Marxist historiography](/wiki/Marxist_historiography "Marxist historiography") developed as a school of historiography influenced by the chief tenets of [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"), including the centrality of [social class](/wiki/Social_class "Social class") and [economic](/wiki/Economic "Economic") constraints in determining historical outcomes. [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") wrote *[The Condition of the Working Class in England in 1844](/wiki/The_Condition_of_the_Working_Class_in_England_in_1844 "The Condition of the Working Class in England in 1844")*, which was salient in creating the [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") impetus in British politics from then on, e.g. the [Fabian Society](/wiki/Fabian_Society "Fabian Society"). [R. H. Tawney](/wiki/R._H._Tawney "R. H. Tawney")'s *The Agrarian Problem in the Sixteenth Century* (1912)[[62]](#cite_note-62) and *Religion and the Rise of Capitalism* (1926), reflected his ethical concerns and preoccupations in [economic history](/wiki/Economic_history "Economic history"). A [circle of historians](/wiki/Communist_Party_Historians_Group "Communist Party Historians Group") inside the [Communist Party of Great Britain](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Great_Britain "Communist Party of Great Britain") (CPGB) formed in 1946 and became a highly influential cluster of British [Marxist historians](/wiki/Marxist_historiography "Marxist historiography"), who contributed to [history from below](/wiki/History_from_below "History from below") and class structure in early capitalist society. Members included [Christopher Hill](/wiki/Christopher_Hill_(historian) "Christopher Hill (historian)"), [Eric Hobsbawm](/wiki/Eric_Hobsbawm "Eric Hobsbawm") and [E. P. Thompson](/wiki/E._P._Thompson "E. P. Thompson").

[World history](/wiki/World_history_(field) "World history (field)"), as a distinct field of historical study, emerged as an independent academic field in the 1980s. It focused on the examination of [history](/wiki/History "History") from a global perspective and looked for common [patterns](/wiki/Pattern "Pattern") that emerged across all cultures. [Arnold J. Toynbee](/wiki/Arnold_J._Toynbee "Arnold J. Toynbee")'s ten-volume *A Study of History*, written between 1933 and 1954, was an important influence on this developing field. He took a comparative topical approach to independent civilizations and demonstrated that they displayed striking parallels in their origin, growth, and decay.[[63]](#cite_note-63) [William H. McNeill](/wiki/William_Hardy_McNeill "William Hardy McNeill") wrote *The Rise of the West* (1965) to improve upon Toynbee by showing how the separate civilizations of Eurasia interacted from the very beginning of their history, borrowing critical skills from one another, and thus precipitating still further change as adjustment between traditional old and borrowed new knowledge and practice became necessary.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

#### Historical editing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Historical editing")]

A new advanced specialty opened in the late 20th century: historical editing. [Edmund Morgan](/wiki/Edmund_Morgan_(historian) "Edmund Morgan (historian)") reports on its emergence in the United States:[[65]](#cite_note-65)

> It required, to begin with, large sums of money. But money has proved easier to recruit than talent. Historians who undertake these large editorial projects must leave the main channel of academic life. They do not teach; they do not write their own books; they do not enjoy long vacations for rumination, reflection, and research on whatever topic interests them at the moment. Instead they must live in unremitting daily pursuit of an individual whose company, whatever his genius, may ultimately begin to pall. Anyone who has edited historical manuscripts knows that it requires as much physical and intellectual labor to prepare a text for publication as it does to write a book of one's own. Indeed, the new editorial projects are far too large for one man. The editor-in-chief, having decided to forego a regular academic career, must entice other scholars to help him; and with the present [high] demand for college teachers, this is no easy task.

## Education and profession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Education and profession")]

Further information: [Professionalization and institutionalization of history](/wiki/Professionalization_and_institutionalization_of_history "Professionalization and institutionalization of history") and [List of historians](/wiki/List_of_historians "List of historians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Peter_Brown_Balzan_Prize_Ceremony_2011.JPG/250px-Peter_Brown_Balzan_Prize_Ceremony_2011.JPG)](/wiki/File:Peter_Brown_Balzan_Prize_Ceremony_2011.JPG)

[Peter R.L Brown](/wiki/Peter_Brown_(historian) "Peter Brown (historian)"), a Princeton historian of [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity") and the [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") period.

An undergraduate history degree is often used as a stepping stone to graduate studies in business or law. Many historians are employed at universities and other facilities for post-secondary education.[[66]](#cite_note-blsgov-66) In addition, it is normal for colleges and universities to require a PhD degree for new full-time hires. A scholarly thesis, such as a doctoral dissertation, is now regarded as the baseline qualification for a professional historian. However, some historians still gain recognition based on published (academic) works and the award of fellowships by academic bodies like the [Royal Historical Society](/wiki/Royal_Historical_Society "Royal Historical Society"). Publication is increasingly required by smaller schools, so graduate papers become journal articles and PhD dissertations become published monographs. The graduate student experience is difficult—those who finish their doctorate in the United States take on average 8 or more years; funding is scarce except at a few very rich universities. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Being a teaching assistant in a course is required in some programs; in others it is a paid opportunity awarded a fraction of the students. Until the 1970s it was rare for graduate programs to teach how to teach; the assumption was that teaching was easy and that learning how to do research was the main mission.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) A critical experience for graduate students is having a mentor who will provide psychological, social, intellectual and professional support, while directing scholarship and providing an introduction to the profession.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Professional historians typically work in colleges and universities, archival centers, government agencies, museums, and as freelance writers and consultants.[[70]](#cite_note-70) In 2010, the [American Historical Association](/wiki/American_Historical_Association "American Historical Association") released its annual job report, which found a 23.8% decline in the number of job advertisements for historians, while the number of new PhDs increased by 17%.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### "Amateur" historians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Historian&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: \"Amateur\" historians")]

[C. Vann Woodward](/wiki/C._Vann_Woodward "C. Vann Woodward") (1908–1999), Sterling Professor of History at Yale University, cautioned that the academicians had themselves abdicated their role as storytellers:

> Professionals do well to apply the term "amateur" with caution to the historian outside their ranks. The word does have deprecatory and patronizing connotations that occasionally backfire. This is especially true of narrative history, which nonprofessionals have all but taken over. The gradual withering of the narrative impulse in favor of the analytical urge among professional academic historians has resulted in a virtual abdication of the oldest and most honored role of the historian, that of storyteller. Having abdicated... the professional is in a poor position to patronize amateurs who fulfill the needed function he has abandoned.[[72]](#cite_note-72)
